messages.content:
In the following VBA code, how would i set the print settings to set the scaling to fit sheet on one page:

Sub UberQuickTotals()
    Dim uberReport As Workbook ' Declare a variable to represent the workbook
    Dim mainSheet As Worksheet ' Declare a variable to represent the source worksheet in the workbook
    Dim lastRow As Long
    Dim dataRange As Range
    Dim targetCell As Range
    Dim printRange As Range
    
    Columns("E").ColumnWidth = 12 ' Adjust column width
    Columns("M").ColumnWidth = 10
    
    ' Determine the last row with data in column L
    lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "L").End(xlUp).Row
    
    ' Define the data range
    Set dataRange = Range("L3:M" & lastRow)
    
    ' Select a cell 1 below the bottom of the selected range in column L
    Set targetCell = Range("L" & lastRow + 1)
    targetCell.Select
    
    ' Enter text into the selected cell
    targetCell.Value = "Total:" ' Change "Text to enter" to your desired text
    
    ' Move the cell focus one column to the right and enter formula
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Select
    Selection.Formula = "=sum(" & dataRange.Address & ")"
    Selection.NumberFormat = "$#,##0.00"
    
    'Sets the range to be printed
    Set printRange = Range("A1:M" & lastRow + 1)
    
    ' Define the print area using the printRange
    ActiveSheet.PageSetup.PrintArea = printRange.Address
    
    ' Set orientation to landscape
    ActiveSheet.PageSetup.Orientation = xlLandscape
    
    ' Temporarily suppress warnings
    Application.DisplayAlerts = False
    
    ' Attempt to print the active sheet
    On Error GoTo PrintError
    ActiveSheet.PrintOut
    
    ' Enable warnings again
    Application.DisplayAlerts = True
    
    ' Print successful
    MsgBox "Printing successful!", vbInformation
    Exit Sub
    
PrintError:
    ' Print failed
    MsgBox "Failed to print. Error: " & Err.Description, vbCritical
    ' Enable warnings again
    Application.DisplayAlerts = True
    
End Sub

To set the scaling to fit the sheet on one page in your VBA code, you will need to adjust the `PageSetup` properties of the worksheet, specifically by setting the `Zoom` property to `False` and then setting the `FitToPagesWide` and `FitToPagesTall` properties accordingly. You want to fit the sheet to 1 page wide by 1 page tall. Here's how you can adjust your code to include this setting right before you set the orientation to landscape:

Below the comment `‘ Set orientation to landscape`, add these lines:

```vba
' Set scaling to fit sheet on one page
With ActiveSheet.PageSetup
    .Zoom = False
    .FitToPagesWide = 1
    .FitToPagesTall = 1
End With
```

So, your code around setting the print settings would look like this:

```vba
' Define the print area using the printRange
ActiveSheet.PageSetup.PrintArea = printRange.Address

' Set scaling to fit sheet on one page
With ActiveSheet.PageSetup
    .Zoom = False
    .FitToPagesWide = 1
    .FitToPagesTall = 1
End With

' Set orientation to landscape
ActiveSheet.PageSetup.Orientation = xlLandscape

' Temporarily suppress warnings
Application.DisplayAlerts = False

' Attempt to print the active sheet
On Error GoTo PrintError
ActiveSheet.PrintOut

' Enable warnings again
Application.DisplayAlerts = True

' Print successful
MsgBox "Printing successful!", vbInformation
Exit Sub
```

This will ensure your print area is scaled to fit on one single page, both vertically and horizontally, when printing from Excel using VBA.
Could you combine multiple lines into one or multiple of those statements together to make the code look better?

Certainly! To make the code more compact and perhaps more readable, you can combine the settings related to `PageSetup` into a single block, including the orientation and scaling adjustments. Here's how you can refactor those lines:

```vba
' Set print settings: orientation and scaling to fit sheet on one page
With ActiveSheet.PageSetup
    .PrintArea = printRange.Address
    .Orientation = xlLandscape
    .Zoom = False
    .FitToPagesWide = 1
    .FitToPagesTall = 1
End With
```

By incorporating these adjustments, the section of your code that prepares the sheet for printing now looks cleaner and is more straightforward to understand at a glance. Note that `.PrintArea` property is also included in this block for consistency, assuming that `printRange` has already been set as we're working within the same sub procedure `UberQuickTotals`.

This compact form not only makes the code more aesthetically pleasing but also groups related properties together, which is a good practice when configuring multiple settings that pertain to a single object (`PageSetup` in this case).
Great! Now is there a way in the code to assign a hotkey to this macro? Or does that have to be done in the macros GUI in excel?

Assigning a hotkey directly within the VBA code of a macro isn't possible in the same way as setting properties or calling methods. Hotkeys or shortcut keys for macros are not set programmatically within the macro's code itself; instead, they can be assigned in a couple of ways outside the code:

1. **Using the Macro Options in the Excel GUI:**
   - Go to the Developer tab and click on Macros.
   - Select the macro you want to assign a hotkey to, then click the Options button.
   - In the Macro Options dialog box, you can specify a letter in the "Shortcut key" box to create a shortcut. The shortcut will be Ctrl+your_letter (or Ctrl+Shift+your_letter if you specify an uppercase letter).
   - Press OK to save the shortcut.

2. **Using the OnKey method:**
   Although you cannot directly embed a hotkey within the macro itself, you can use the `Application.OnKey` method to assign a procedure to run with a specific key combination from another macro or the `Workbook_Open` event. 

Here is how you could use `Application.OnKey` in the `Workbook_Open` event to assign a shortcut key to your `UberQuickTotals` macro. This code should be placed in the `ThisWorkbook` object within VBA:

```vba
Private Sub Workbook_Open()
    ' Assign Ctrl+Shift+U to run UberQuickTotals
    Application.OnKey "^+U", "UberQuickTotals"
End Sub
```

And to remove the shortcut when the workbook is closed, you could use the `Workbook_BeforeClose` event:

```vba
Private Sub Workbook_BeforeClose(Cancel As Boolean)
    ' Remove the shortcut for UberQuickTotals
    Application.OnKey "^+U"
End Sub
```

Remember, the `OnKey` approach works globally within Excel while the workbook that assigned the key is open, so it can potentially override any existing shortcuts and affect other workbooks. Always choose unique and intuitively safe key combinations to avoid conflicts with other shortcuts or macros.

In the following VBA code, is it possible to add a button to the printing successful window to exit the currently open excel workbook?

Sub UberQuickTotals()
    Dim uberReport As Workbook ' Declare a variable to represent the workbook
    Dim mainSheet As Worksheet ' Declare a variable to represent the source worksheet in the workbook
    Dim lastRow As Long
    Dim dataRange As Range
    Dim targetCell As Range
    Dim printRange As Range
    
    Columns("E").ColumnWidth = 12 ' Adjust column width
    Columns("M").ColumnWidth = 10
    
    ' Determine the last row with data in column L
    lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "L").End(xlUp).Row
    
    ' Define the data range
    Set dataRange = Range("L3:M" & lastRow)
    
    ' Select a cell 1 below the bottom of the selected range in column L
    Set targetCell = Range("L" & lastRow + 1)
    targetCell.Select
    
    ' Enter text into the selected cell
    targetCell.Value = "Total:" ' Change "Text to enter" to your desired text
    
    ' Move the cell focus one column to the right and enter formula
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Select
    Selection.Formula = "=sum(" & dataRange.Address & ")"
    Selection.NumberFormat = "$#,##0.00"
    
    'Sets the range to be printed
    Set printRange = Range("A1:M" & lastRow + 1)
    
    ' Set print settings: orientation and scaling to fit sheet on one page
    With ActiveSheet.PageSetup
        .PrintArea = printRange.Address
        .Orientation = xlLandscape
        .Zoom = False
        .FitToPagesWide = 1
        .FitToPagesTall = 1
    End With
    
    ' Temporarily suppress warnings
    Application.DisplayAlerts = False
    
    ' Attempt to print the active sheet
    On Error GoTo PrintError
    ActiveSheet.PrintOut
    
    ' Enable warnings again
    Application.DisplayAlerts = True
    
    ' Print successful
    MsgBox "Printing successful!", vbInformation
    Exit Sub
    
PrintError:
    ' Print failed
    MsgBox "Failed to print. Error: " & Err.Description, vbCritical
    ' Enable warnings again
    Application.DisplayAlerts = True
    
End Sub

In VBA, the simplest way to present users with a choice after an operation (such as exiting the workbook after printing) is through a message box that asks them whether they want to perform the additional action. Unfortunately, the default `MsgBox` function in VBA doesn't allow for custom buttons beyond the pre-set styles (like Yes/No, Ok/Cancel, etc.). 

However, you can use one of these pre-set button combinations to ask the user if they would like to close the workbook after confirming the printing was successful. For example, you could use a `vbYesNo` message box and check the user's response:

```vba
' Print successful
Dim response As Integer
response = MsgBox("Printing successful! Do you want to close this workbook?", vbInformation + vbYesNo)

If response = vbYes Then
    ThisWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
End If
```

In this snippet, after the print operation is confirmed successful, a message box appears with both "Yes" and "No" options. If the user clicks "Yes," the workbook will close without saving changes. If you want to prompt the user to save any changes before closing, you can change `SaveChanges:=False` to `SaveChanges:=True` or omit the `SaveChanges` parameter to prompt the user.

Remember, closing the workbook programmatically will stop any subsequent code from running because the environment that the code runs in is being closed. Ensure this is the last action you want to perform in your procedure.

Also note, showing a dialog to close the workbook right after reporting successful printing might not always be the expected behavior for the user if they have not saved their work or if they wish to do additional tasks in the workbook. Use this feature considerately.

Why does this line give me an error, specifically a compile error saying expected list seperator or )

response = MsgBox(“Printing succesful, Do you want to close the window?”, vbInformation + vbYesNo)

The issue you're encountering with the `MsgBox` function line giving a compile error, specifically saying expected list separator or `)`, is likely due to the type of quotation marks used in your code. In VBA, and programming in general, it's important to use straight quotation marks (`"`) instead of curly or smart quotation marks (`“` and `”`), which are typically used in word processing software for styling purposes.

The line causing the error appears to use curly quotation marks:

```vba
response = MsgBox(“Printing successful, Do you want to close the window?”, vbInformation + vbYesNo)
```

To fix this error, replace the curly quotation marks with straight ones, like so:

```vba
response = MsgBox("Printing successful, Do you want to close the window?", vbInformation + vbYesNo)
```

This should resolve the compile error you're experiencing. In programming, it's crucial to stick with straight quotation marks for indicating strings or text values. The use of curly quotation marks is a common issue when copying code from websites or documents that have formatted text for readability or style, not for code compatibility.

can you add an else to this to make it not display the print warning if the user selects no:
If response = vbYes Then
        ThisWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
    End If

To prevent the display of a print warning if the user selects "No" when prompted about closing the workbook, and assuming you're referring to managing the response without showing any additional warning or action when "No" is selected, the `If` statement you've provided indeed does exactly that — performing an action only if the answer is "Yes" (without needing an explicit `Else` for the "No" choice, especially if no further action or message is required on selecting "No").

However, if you intended to perform some action or display some information when the user selects "No", you could indeed add an `Else` part to your conditional statement. Here's how you could structure it with an `Else`, just for illustrating purposes, even if in this case the `Else` does nothing:

```vba
' Print successful
Dim response As Integer
response = MsgBox("Printing successful! Do you want to close this workbook?", vbInformation + vbYesNo)

If response = vbYes Then
    ThisWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
Else
    ' No action needed for 'No', but this is where it would go if it did.
    ' Optionally, you could display a message, log something, etc.
    ' MsgBox "The workbook will remain open.", vbInformation ' Example message
End If
```

In the context provided, where no action is taken on a "No" response, the `Else` clause could be considered redundant. Therefore, including an `Else` clause without any instructions inside would not change the behavior of your code in this situation. The initial code snippet you provided is already arranged to ignore the print warning if the user selects "No", effectively closing the workbook only on a "Yes" response and doing nothing otherwise.
could we return or something similar? like if the else triggers then exit out of the code instantly

If you want your VBA procedure to stop executing further when the user selects "No" in your `MsgBox` prompt without having an explicit task to perform, you can use `Exit Sub`. This tells VBA to exit the subroutine immediately, skipping any code that follows. This approach is useful if you have additional code in your subroutine that you do not want to execute after the user decides not to close the workbook.

Here’s how you could structure your conditional to include an early exit:

```vba
' Print successful
Dim response As Integer
response = MsgBox("Printing successful! Do you want to close this workbook?", vbInformation + vbYesNo)

If response = vbYes Then
    ThisWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
Else
    ' Exit the subroutine if 'No' is selected, preventing any further code execution
    Exit Sub
End If

' Any code here will only execute if the user has not been prompted with msgbox or
' if they select "Yes" and the workbook closing command somehow fails (which is unlikely in this immediate context)
```

In this modified version, if the user selects 'No', `Exit Sub` is reached, which causes the subroutine to terminate immediately. Consequently, no further code within this subroutine will be executed. This is particularly useful in scenarios where running the remainder of the subroutine is contingent upon the user's decision to proceed (in this case, affirmatively closing the workbook or not).

So this line of code closes the PERSONAL file because my macro is stored there. How do i get it to close the currently open workbook that the macro is running in: ThisWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False

The reason your macro is closing the `PERSONAL.XLSB` workbook (where it's stored) instead of the currently active workbook is due to the use of `ThisWorkbook`. In the context of VBA, `ThisWorkbook` refers to the workbook where the VBA code resides. If your code is in a module within your `PERSONAL.XLSB` macro workbook, then `ThisWorkbook` points to `PERSONAL.XLSB`.

To close the currently active workbook (the one that you are working on, not the one containing the macro), you should use `ActiveWorkbook` instead. `ActiveWorkbook` refers to the workbook that is currently active in the Excel interface, which may or may not be the same as `ThisWorkbook`, depending on where the focus is when your macro runs.

Here's how you can modify your code to close the currently active workbook without saving changes:

```vba
' Print successful
Dim response As Integer
response = MsgBox("Printing successful! Do you want to close this workbook?", vbInformation + vbYesNo)

If response = vbYes Then
    ActiveWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
Else
    ' Exit the subroutine if 'No' is selected
    Exit Sub
End If
```

Using `ActiveWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False` ensures that the workbook you are actively working in is the one that will be closed without saving any changes. Be cautious when using `ActiveWorkbook`, especially with macros that close workbooks, as it's easy to accidentally close the wrong workbook if you aren't keeping track of which workbook is active. 

It's a good practice to validate that the active workbook is indeed the one you intend to close. If your macro should always target a specific workbook other than where the macro is stored, consider using more explicit references to the workbook you want to work with, such as by its name or index, to prevent unintended closures.

So i have the following code to exit the excel window. But it still keeps a excel window open at the end but with nothing open in it. How do i get it to fully exit excel:
ActiveWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
        ThisWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False
        Exit Sub

If you're looking to close Excel completely after running your macro, including closing all open workbooks and the Excel application itself, you should proceed with caution. This is because forcefully closing Excel will not only end your macro's execution but also close any and all workbooks the user may have open, potentially leading to lost work if not everything is saved.

However, understanding that there are cases where this might be exactly what you're aiming to do, here's how you can close the entire Excel application through VBA:

```vba
ActiveWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False

' Assuming you no longer need to close ThisWorkbook since it'll close with the application
' ThisWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False

Application.Quit
```

Adding `Application.Quit` at the end of your script instructs VBA to terminate the Excel application entirely. This will close all open workbooks and the Excel window. If there are unsaved workbooks (besides the one you've already closed with `SaveChanges:=False`), Excel usually prompts the user to save changes, depending on your Excel settings and how the macro is executed. 

Keep in mind that closing the entire Excel application might not always be user-friendly, especially if the user has other workbooks open for different tasks. It's good practice to include clear warnings or prompts within your macro, to ensure the user understands that Excel will close and to give them a chance to cancel the operation or save their work in other open workbooks.

For a more cautious approach, before making any decision to quit the application, checking if there are any other workbooks open besides the one being actively closed can add a layer of safety:

```vba
ActiveWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False

' Check if there are no other workbooks open before quitting
If Application.Workbooks.Count = 0 Then
    ' Only quit Excel if there are no other workbooks open
    Application.Quit
Else
    MsgBox "There are other workbooks open.", vbInformation
End If
```

This way, you reduce the risk of inadvertently closing workbooks the user might still be working on.
